rubric_identity.one_sentence_purpose: Evaluate CVs for senior AI architect roles emphasizing production platform design, governance-aware delivery, stakeholder translation, and player-coach technical leadership without executive management inflation.
rubric_identity.core_persona: Senior AI architect or solutions architect who designs and delivers production-grade AI platforms, translates business priorities into deployable architecture, mentors engineers, and owns governance and reliability outcomes as a hands-on architect or player-coach rather than a formal people manager.
job_overlay_notes: Adjust RAG/retrieval weighting based on whether JD explicitly mentions retrieval patterns or conversational AI systems.
Increase governance/guardrails weight for roles mentioning compliance, regulated industries, or enterprise AI deployment.
Weight multi-cloud evidence higher for consultancy or systems-integrator roles versus single-vendor enterprise positions.
For player-coach roles, weight mentoring and architectural guidance; for pure IC roles, weight hands-on implementation depth.
KSA/UAE regional roles may weight stakeholder management and enterprise collaboration higher than pure technical depth.

what_good_looks_like: Uses architect-family title variants aligned to market sample: Solutions Architect, AI Architect, AI Solution Architect, GenAI Solutions Architect.
Includes strongest keywords 2-3 times across headline/profile/experience: AI architecture, solutions architecture, platform design, stakeholder management, governance, RAG.
Includes secondary keywords 1-2 times: MLOps, multi-cloud, Azure, LangChain, deployment, reliability, cost optimization, monitoring, Kubernetes.
Avoids keyword stuffing while ensuring coverage of table-stakes signals: platform design, production deployment, stakeholder translation.
Places production AI platform design evidence immediately after title in headline or summary.
common_failures: Uses generic engineering or leadership titles without architect-family positioning.
Omits core keywords like governance, guardrails, RAG, or stakeholder management entirely.
Overloads with tool names without architecture or delivery context.
Uses executive titles (Head of AI, Director, VP) that misalign with category role shape.
Buries strongest AI architecture evidence in later sections rather than headline/summary.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: If JD emphasizes RAG or retrieval systems, ensure RAG, vector databases, and retrieval appear prominently.
If JD names specific cloud platforms, include those platform names 1-2 times if evidenced.
If JD emphasizes governance or compliance, foreground guardrails, governance, and compliance keywords.
Mirror JD terminology for orchestration patterns: agents, workflows, pipelines as used in posting.
company_domain_overrides: For consultancies or SIs: emphasize solution design, proposal support, client-facing architecture.
For product companies: emphasize platform ownership, scalability, production operations.
For regulated industries: include compliance-specific terms only if directly evidenced.
region_overrides: KSA/UAE roles may value enterprise stakeholder collaboration language more than pure technical depth.
Do not claim Arabic proficiency unless directly evidenced on CV.
Do not assume Azure-native requirements; multi-cloud framing is stronger in sample.

9_10: Headline uses safe architect-family title with production AI platform design proof.
All strongest keywords present 2-3 times with natural distribution across sections.
Secondary keywords present 1-2 times each where evidenced.
No keyword stuffing; reading flows naturally while covering ATS requirements.
JD-specific terminology mirrored accurately throughout.
7_8: Architect-family title present but platform design proof not immediately adjacent.
Most strongest keywords present but some at suboptimal frequency.
Minor gaps in secondary keyword coverage.
Generally good ATS optimization with 1-2 missed opportunities.
5_6: Title positioning unclear or uses generic software/engineering framing.
Several strongest keywords missing or appearing only once.
Significant gaps in secondary keyword coverage.
ATS optimization inconsistent across sections.
0_4: No architect-family positioning; uses executive or research titles.
Most category-critical keywords absent.
Heavy keyword stuffing that damages readability.
Fundamental mismatch between CV keywords and category requirements.

red_flags: Executive titles: Head of AI, Director of AI, Chief AI Officer, VP AI.
Research titles: Principal Scientist, Research Scientist when category requires architect.
Tool laundry lists without architecture context.
Claiming multi-cloud as primary positioning without multi-cloud platform evidence.
Omitting stakeholder management, governance, or platform design keywords entirely.

what_good_looks_like: Architecture and delivery outcomes quantified: users served, systems integrated, deployment timelines, adoption rates.
Scale and reliability metrics present: uptime, incident reduction, latency improvement, throughput.
Governance outcomes evidenced: audit success, compliance coverage, guardrail implementation scope.
Cost and efficiency improvements quantified where applicable.
Stakeholder impact shown: executives aligned, roadmaps approved, architecture decisions adopted.
common_failures: Vague architecture claims without deployment or adoption evidence.
Missing scale metrics for platform work.
No reliability or operational quality evidence.
Governance framed as buzzword rather than implemented outcome.
Stakeholder work described without concrete results.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: If JD emphasizes scale, prioritize throughput, user count, and system integration metrics.
If JD emphasizes reliability, prioritize uptime, incident reduction, and SLO attainment.
If JD emphasizes governance, prioritize audit outcomes, compliance coverage, guardrail scope.
If JD emphasizes cost, prioritize cloud cost reduction and efficiency gains.
company_domain_overrides: For consultancies: include client delivery outcomes, proposal support impact, solution estimation accuracy.
For enterprises: include cross-team adoption, platform consolidation, operating cost improvements.
For startups: include speed-to-production, MVP delivery timelines, iteration velocity.
region_overrides: KSA/UAE enterprise roles may weight stakeholder alignment and executive adoption metrics higher.
Regional roles may value revenue-protection or compliance-risk-reduction outcomes.

9_10: Every major achievement includes specific, verifiable metrics.
Metrics span architecture, delivery, scale, reliability, and governance dimensions.
Impact clearly tied to architect ownership, not team or org attribution.
Stakeholder outcomes quantified: executives aligned, roadmaps approved, decisions adopted.
Story format (ARIS/STAR) used to connect problem, design, implementation, and result.
7_8: Most achievements include metrics but 1-2 lack specificity.
Good coverage across impact dimensions with minor gaps.
Attribution generally clear with occasional ambiguity.
Stakeholder impact present but less quantified than technical outcomes.
5_6: Several achievements lack metrics or use vague quantifiers.
Coverage skewed toward one impact dimension (e.g., only delivery, no governance).
Attribution unclear; hard to distinguish individual vs team contribution.
Stakeholder impact claims present but unsupported.
0_4: Most achievements lack any metrics.
Impact claims generic: 'improved systems', 'designed architecture'.
No evidence of production deployment or adoption.
No governance, reliability, or stakeholder outcomes shown.

red_flags: Unsourced metrics that cannot be traced to CV evidence.
Round-number inflation (10x, 100x) without supporting context.
Revenue or P&L claims in a category where these are unsafe.
Team or org outcomes attributed as individual architect ownership.
Prototype or POC work framed as production impact.

what_good_looks_like: Platform design evidence present as table-stakes requirement (96.7% of roles).
Production deployment and delivery outcomes shown, not just architecture design.
Stakeholder management and collaboration evidence present (60% of roles).
Governance and guardrails evidence for GenAI safety (50% of roles).
RAG or retrieval-centered architecture experience where relevant (40% of roles).
MLOps, CI/CD, monitoring, and operational tooling familiarity demonstrated.
Multi-cloud or cloud platform architecture evidence present.
common_failures: Missing platform design evidence despite it being category-defining.
No production deployment proof; only design or experimentation shown.
Stakeholder and collaboration dimensions absent.
Governance framed as compliance buzzword rather than implemented architecture.
Pure modeling focus without architecture integration evidence.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: Weight RAG/retrieval higher if JD explicitly mentions conversational AI, knowledge systems, or document processing.
Weight governance higher if JD mentions compliance, regulated industries, or enterprise deployment.
Weight multi-cloud higher for consultancy or SI roles versus single-vendor enterprise.
Weight player-coach mentoring if JD mentions technical leadership or team guidance.
Weight customer-facing solution shaping if JD mentions proposals, pre-sales, or client engagement.
company_domain_overrides: Financial services: weight governance, compliance, audit outcomes higher.
Consultancies: weight solution estimation, proposal support, client communication.
Product companies: weight platform ownership, scalability, production reliability.
Startups: weight end-to-end delivery speed and iteration capability.
region_overrides: KSA/UAE roles may emphasize enterprise stakeholder management over pure technical depth.
Do not assume Arabic language requirements without JD evidence.
Regional government or sovereign cloud requirements require direct evidence.

9_10: Platform design evidence with production deployment outcomes clearly shown.
All category table-stakes covered: architecture, delivery, stakeholder, cloud.
Differentiators present where relevant: RAG, governance, scale, reliability.
JD-specific requirements directly addressed with evidenced achievements.
Role shape (senior IC / player-coach) correctly framed for the specific JD.
7_8: Platform design and production evidence present with minor gaps.
Most table-stakes covered; 1-2 differentiators missing or weak.
JD requirements generally addressed but not all with direct evidence.
Role shape mostly appropriate with minor misalignment.
5_6: Platform design claimed but production evidence weak.
Several table-stakes missing or poorly evidenced.
JD alignment inconsistent; some requirements unaddressed.
Role shape unclear or partially misaligned.
0_4: No platform design evidence despite category requirement.
Most table-stakes missing.
Fundamental mismatch between CV focus and JD requirements.
Role shape (executive, researcher) contradicts category.

red_flags: Pure research or experimentation focus without production architecture.
Executive management framing when JD seeks senior IC or player-coach.
Tool-centric positioning without architecture context.
Missing stakeholder management evidence in stakeholder-heavy JDs.
Claiming multi-cloud expertise without multi-cloud platform evidence.

what_good_looks_like: Architectural authority demonstrated through system ownership and platform design decisions.
Stakeholder translation: translating business priorities into deployable architecture.
Technical governance ownership: architecture reviews, standards, guardrails implementation.
Player-coach leadership: mentoring engineers, promoting architectural integrity, guiding teams.
Enterprise collaboration: working with product, security, DevOps, and enterprise architects.
Risk communication: transparent communication of technical decisions and tradeoffs to business stakeholders.
common_failures: Framing presence as C-suite, boardroom, or P&L ownership in an architect category.
Claiming direct reports, hiring, or performance management ownership.
Using executive vocabulary (strategic, visionary, transformational) without architect context.
Missing stakeholder translation evidence despite category emphasis.
Confusing player-coach with formal management.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: If JD emphasizes technical leadership, weight architectural mentoring and standards ownership.
If JD emphasizes enterprise integration, weight cross-functional collaboration evidence.
If JD mentions client engagement, weight solution shaping and proposal support.
Pure IC JDs: de-weight mentoring; weight hands-on architectural depth instead.
company_domain_overrides: Consultancies: weight client-facing communication and solution estimation.
Enterprises: weight cross-functional alignment and governance ownership.
Startups: weight autonomous architectural decision-making and delivery ownership.
region_overrides: KSA/UAE enterprise roles may weight formal stakeholder management higher.
Regional roles may value relationship-building and trust communication.

9_10: Clear architectural authority through platform design ownership and technical governance.
Stakeholder translation demonstrated with specific examples and outcomes.
Player-coach leadership evidenced: mentoring, promoting engineers, guiding architecture.
Enterprise collaboration shown across product, security, DevOps, and business partners.
No management inflation; leadership framed as architectural guidance, not org management.
7_8: Good architectural authority evidence with minor gaps in stakeholder translation.
Player-coach leadership present but less specifically evidenced.
Enterprise collaboration shown but not across full breadth of stakeholders.
Generally appropriate framing with occasional management language.
5_6: Architectural authority claimed but weakly evidenced.
Stakeholder translation unclear or missing.
Leadership framing mixed; some management inflation present.
Enterprise collaboration evidence sparse.
0_4: No architectural authority evidence; pure implementation focus.
Stakeholder translation absent.
Heavy management inflation: direct reports, hiring, P&L claims.
Executive framing inappropriate for architect category.

red_flags: Unsupported people-management framing: direct reports, hiring, performance management.
Executive titles claimed: Head of AI, Director, VP, Chief.
Budget/P&L ownership claims in a category with 0% management signals.
C-suite or boardroom language without architect context.
Org-building claims when org-building is only 13.3% in sample.

what_good_looks_like: All metrics traceable to CV evidence; no invented numbers.
Title claims match actual role titles held.
Scope claims (users, systems, scale) supported by role evidence.
Technology claims match actual implementation experience, not exposure.
Leadership claims match evidenced scope: mentoring vs management.
Regional/language claims only when directly evidenced.
common_failures: Fabricated or inflated metrics without CV support.
Title inflation: claiming Director when held Senior role.
Scope inflation: claiming org-wide impact for team project.
Technology inflation: claiming expertise from conference attendance.
Management inflation: claiming direct reports when mentoring peers.
Regional claims: Arabic fluency, sovereign compliance without evidence.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: Scrutinize multi-cloud claims if JD requires multi-cloud but CV shows single platform.
Verify governance/compliance claims against actual regulatory frameworks implemented.
Check production fine-tuning claims which are frequently unsupported.
Verify RAG implementation depth vs superficial exposure.
company_domain_overrides: Regulated industry claims require specific framework evidence (GDPR, banking regulations).
Enterprise scale claims require user count, transaction volume, or system scope evidence.
Consultancy claims require client engagement evidence, not just internal work.
region_overrides: Arabic language claims must have direct CV evidence.
Azure-native enterprise scale claims require specific platform and scale documentation.
Sovereign or regional compliance claims require direct implementation evidence.

9_10: All claims traceable to specific CV evidence.
No title, scope, or metric inflation detected.
Technology claims match implementation depth, not exposure.
Leadership scope accurately reflects mentoring vs management evidence.
No unsupported regional, language, or compliance claims.
7_8: Most claims evidenced with 1-2 minor unverifiable statements.
No significant inflation detected.
Technology and leadership claims generally accurate.
Minor ambiguity in scope attribution.
5_6: Several claims lack clear evidence.
Some inflation detected in scope or impact claims.
Technology claims exceed implementation evidence.
Leadership scope unclear or potentially inflated.
0_4: Multiple fabricated or unsourced claims.
Clear title or scope inflation.
Technology claims contradict CV evidence.
Management claims unsupported by any evidence.
Regional or compliance claims fabricated.

red_flags: Unsourced metrics: percentages, user counts, revenue figures without CV backing.
Fabricated titles: executive titles never held.
PhD or publication claims for a category with 0% research requirements.
Direct report counts unsupported by role evidence.
Production fine-tuning, RLHF, or custom embedding training claims without implementation evidence.
Arabic fluency claims without language evidence.
Azure-native enterprise scale claims without platform documentation.
Budget/P&L ownership in a 0% budget-ownership category.
GTM or channel-partner ownership claims without pre-sales evidence.
Vector database indexing or GPU autoscaling claims without implementation evidence.

pass_criteria: Platform design evidence present with production deployment outcomes.
At least one quantified architecture or delivery achievement.
Stakeholder management or collaboration evidence present.
Architect-family title positioning used (Solutions Architect, AI Architect, or variant).
Cloud platform architecture evidence present (AWS, Azure, GCP, or multi-cloud).
fail_conditions: No platform design evidence despite 96.7% category requirement.
No production deployment outcomes; only design or experimentation.
Complete absence of stakeholder or collaboration evidence.
Executive or research title positioning instead of architect family.
No cloud or infrastructure architecture evidence.

pass_criteria: No unsupported people-management claims (hiring 0%, performance management 0% in category).
No executive titles claimed without matching role evidence.
No Arabic language claims without CV evidence.
No Azure-native enterprise scale claims without platform documentation.
No budget/P&L/revenue ownership claims in this architect category.
No production fine-tuning or RLHF claims without implementation evidence.
No fabricated metrics or unsourced numbers.
fail_conditions: Claims direct reports, hiring, or performance management ownership.
Uses executive titles: Head of AI, Director, VP, Chief.
Claims Arabic fluency without language evidence.
Claims Azure-native regulated enterprise AI architecture without specific evidence.
Claims budget, P&L, or revenue ownership.
Claims production fine-tuning, RLHF, or custom embeddings without evidence.
Includes metrics that cannot be traced to CV evidence.

pass_criteria: Role shape matches senior IC (90%) or player-coach (50%) positioning.
Leadership framed as architectural authority and mentoring, not org management.
No research-heavy or PhD-required framing (both 0% in category).
Stakeholder translation present, not executive decision-making authority.
Production architecture focus, not experimentation or prototype focus.
fail_conditions: Executive management framing: org design, budget ownership, P&L.
Research scientist or academic framing inappropriate for architect role.
Heavy people-management focus contradicts senior IC / player-coach shape.
Pure experimentation focus without production architecture evidence.
Missing stakeholder translation despite 60% category emphasis.

scoring_guidance.how_to_interpret_executive_presence: For AI Architect category, interpret executive_presence as architectural authority and system ownership. Reward platform design decisions, governance ownership, stakeholder translation, and player-coach mentoring. DO NOT reward or expect direct reports, C-suite access, P&L ownership, or boardroom presentations. Penalize management inflation; the category shows hiring 0%, performance management 0%, org-building 13.3%.
scoring_guidance.how_to_penalize_unsupported_claims: Apply immediate 2-point penalty per dimension for any unsourced metric or fabricated claim. Apply gate failure for systematic inflation (3+ unsupported claims) or any category-specific unsafe claim: executive titles, Arabic fluency, Azure-native scale, budget/P&L, direct reports, production fine-tuning without evidence. Verify all numbers against CV evidence before scoring.
scoring_guidance.how_to_handle_category_vs_job_tradeoffs: Category requirements are baseline; JD requirements are overlay. If JD requires something the category marks optional (e.g., specific compliance framework), weight it higher for that specific evaluation. If JD de-emphasizes a category table-stake (rare), maintain minimum coverage but reduce weight. Never allow JD to override category unsafe-claim rules.

evidence_ledger.rubric_rule: Platform design is table-stakes requirement appearing in 96.7% of roles
Senior IC (90%) and player-coach (50%) are dominant role shapes; hiring and performance management are 0%
Stakeholder management appears in 60% of roles as core requirement
Guardrails/governance appears in 50% of roles as differentiator
RAG/retrieval patterns appear in 40% of roles as differentiator
Solutions Architect (63.3%) and AI Architect (36.7%) are safe title families
Unsafe claims include Arabic fluency, Azure-native scale, budget/P&L, direct reports, production fine-tuning without evidence
Research-heavy and PhD requirements are both 0% in category; avoid research framing
Multi-cloud preference (40%) is stronger than Azure-only (20%) in sample
Baseline CV supports architect positioning but current headline underrepresents AI architecture evidence
Commander-4 and Lantern projects provide production AI architecture evidence including RAG, guardrails, and gateway design
Mentoring 10+ engineers and promoting 3 to leads supports player-coach positioning, not formal management
